prompt_queue.prompt: Bootstrap the repository's non-technical, user-facing living documentation from source code. Treat this as a first-run documentation bootstrap. Study README.md, docs/**, source files, UI strings, commands, configuration, tests, examples, issues, and pull requests. Begin by building a source inventory of user-visible features, setup paths, configuration options, permissions or requirements, common tasks, operational expectations, compatibility boundaries, troubleshooting paths, FAQ-worthy behaviors, tests or source areas that prove those behaviors, and the existing documentation information architecture. Use that inventory to create a clean documentation surface that helps a new user understand the product and complete common tasks confidently. Preserve or improve the repository's existing docs taxonomy, index convention, and topic grouping; create audience folders such as user/ only when the repository already uses that convention or the consumer prompt explicitly asks for it. Choose a committed scope that fits the run budget, then place additional inventory items in a future coverage section as plain text with reasons. Organize the output into a concise docs index plus separate digestible task/topic pages whose hierarchy mirrors the product's user-facing architecture, feature areas, and natural task flows. Use clear titles, bounded scope, parent/child relationships, and useful cross-links so readers can move from overview to focused instructions without reading a monolithic page. Include practical details for user-facing surfaces: prerequisites, setup steps, configuration examples, expected outcomes, permission requirements, troubleshooting cues, and behavior notes grounded in source or tests. Write topic pages first, then write or update the index last so navigation reflects committed files. Make the index describe the committed documentation scope and link only to committed pages. Write every linked documentation page before opening a pull request, and verify the final index and page links resolve to committed files. Reconcile the written docs against the source inventory before opening the pull request: each major inventory item should be documented or explicitly listed as future coverage with the reason. Prefer documentation that readers can navigate and understand quickly over audit artifacts. Use create_or_update_github_file before create_github_pull_request.
